mnemonics.title: ACID Properties
mnemonics.content: Remember ACID as 'All Children In Desperation' - Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability

mnemonics.title: ARIES Phases
mnemonics.content: Remember ARIES as 'Always Recreate Important Entries Systematically' - Analysis, Redo, Undo

mnemonics.title: WAL Protocol
mnemonics.content: WAL = Write Ahead Log - Always write LOG before DATA modification

mnemonics.title: Recovery Order
mnemonics.content: Remember: COMMIT = REDO needed, ABORT = UNDO needed

acronyms.expansion: Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics
acronyms.usage: Standard recovery algorithm used by IBM DB2, Oracle, and other databases
Protocol ensuring log records are written before data modifications
Unique identifier for each log record in sequential order
Table tracking modified pages not yet written to disk
Table maintaining status and LSN of all active transactions
Unique sequence number identifying each log record

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with states: Active → Partially Committed → Committed, with Abort arrow from Active/Partially Committed to Aborted → Active (for new transaction)
Box 1: Failure occurs → Box 2: Analyze (read log) → Box 3: Redo committed → Box 4: Undo uncommitted → Box 5: Consistent state restored
Draw table with columns: LSN | Transaction ID | Type | Previous LSN | UndoNextLSN | Data
Vertical timeline: 1) BEGIN transaction 2) Write LOG to disk 3) Write DATA to buffer 4) COMMIT (write commit record)
Two columns: PageID | RecLSN (recovery LSN - earliest log record that might need redo for this page)

analogies.analogy: Like a bank ATM transaction - either cash is dispensed AND account debited, OR neither happens. No partial states.
Like saving a document - once saved (committed), it persists even if computer crashes.
Like keeping a diary of actions BEFORE doing them - if something goes wrong, you can recreate what should have happened.
Like editing a copy of a document while keeping the original safe - only replace original when done.
Like auto-save in video games - creates a known good state to return to if something goes wrong.
Undo is like Ctrl+Z (reverse action); Redo is like reapplying an action from history.
Like a manifest or checklist tracking all ongoing activities and their status.
